title: Angles Around a Point Sum to 360°
description: Recognize that the measures of all angles around a single point add to 360 degrees.
condition: Multiple angles share a common vertex and form a full circle around a point.
response: Set up an equation where the sum of the angle measures equals 360°.
label: Principle / Model
example:
If angles around a point are $a + b + c + 27$, then
$$a + b + c + 27 = 360.$$

title: Area of a Composite Figure
description: Find the area of an irregular polygon by decomposing it into simpler shapes and summing their areas.
condition: Given a composite 2D figure with known side lengths that can be partitioned into rectangles, triangles, or trapezoids.
response: Decompose the figure, compute each part’s area, and add them to find total area.
label: Principle
example: If a figure is split into a rectangle of area $1.5$ ft$^2$ and a trapezoid of area $4.5$ ft$^2$, total area $= 1.5 + 4.5 = 6$ ft$^2$.

title: Area of a Trapezoid
description: Compute the area of a trapezoid using the average of the two bases times the height.
condition: Given a trapezoid with bases b1 and b2 and height h.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Height must be perpendicular to bases.

title: Complementary Angles Sum to 90°
description: Identify that two angles forming a right angle are complementary and sum to 90 degrees.
condition: Two adjacent angles together form a right angle (indicated by a square corner or 90°).
response: Write an equation adding the two angle measures and set equal to 90°.
label: Principle / Model
example:
If angles are $a$ and $27^\circ$ forming a right angle, then
$$a + 27 = 90.$$

title: Construct Triangle from Three Side Lengths
description: Given three side lengths that satisfy the triangle inequality, construct a triangle by drawing one side and locating the third vertex using the other two lengths.
condition: Given three positive side lengths that can form a triangle.
response: Draw one side as a base, use the other two lengths to locate the third vertex (e.g., with arcs), and connect endpoints to form a triangle.
label: Production / Skill
example: Given side lengths 3 in, 4 in, and 6 in: Draw a 6-inch segment. Draw an arc of radius 3 in from one endpoint and an arc of radius 4 in from the other. Their intersection determines the third vertex.

title: Lateral Surface Area from Perimeter
description: Calculate lateral surface area of a prism using perimeter of base times height.
condition: Given a prism with known base perimeter and height.
label: Principle
example: If perimeter $=11$ ft and height $=3$ ft, lateral area $=11 \times 3 = 33$ ft$^2$.

title: Linear Pair are Supplementary
description: Angles that form a straight line sum to 180 degrees.
condition: Two adjacent angles form a straight line.
response: Set their measures to sum to $180^\circ$.
label: Principle

title: Non-Included Angle (SSA) Can Produce Different Triangles
description: When two sides and a non-included angle are given, more than one distinct triangle may satisfy the measures.
condition: Given two side lengths and an angle that is not between them (SSA configuration).
response: Conclude that different (non-congruent) triangles may be possible.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given sides 2 in and 5 in and a $30^\circ$ angle not between them, the third vertex can lie in different positions, producing different triangles.

title: Recognize Surface Area Context
description: Determine that a situation involving covering or material on the outside requires surface area.
condition: A real-world problem asks for covering, wrapping, painting, or material needed for the outside of a 3D object.
response: Identify surface area as the required quantity.
label: Concept
example: To find how much wrapping paper is needed to wrap a present, use surface area.

title: Recognize Volume Context
description: Determine that a situation involving capacity or space inside a 3D object requires volume.
condition: A real-world problem asks for how much a container can hold or how much space is inside a 3D object.
response: Identify volume as the required quantity.
label: Concept
example: To find how much water fills a tank, use volume.
notes: Includes capacity and cubic unit contexts.

title: SAS Determines a Unique Triangle
description: If two side lengths and the included angle are fixed, exactly one triangle (up to congruence) can be formed.
condition: Given two side lengths and the angle between them (SAS).
response: Conclude that exactly one unique triangle can be formed.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given sides 2 in and 5 in with the included angle $30^\circ$, only one triangle can be formed.

title: Scaled Polygons Preserve Angles
description: Recognize that scaled copies of polygons have equal corresponding angle measures.
condition: A polygon is described as a scaled copy of another.
label: Concept
example: If two triangles are scaled copies, and one has angles $30^\circ, 80^\circ, 70^\circ$, the other has the same angle measures.

title: Solve One-Step Equation by Subtraction
description: Solve an equation of the form x + k = c by subtracting k from both sides.
condition: An equation where a variable has a constant added to it (e.g., x + k = c).
response: Subtract the constant from both sides to isolate the variable.
label: Rule / Plan

title: SSS Determines a Unique Triangle
description: If all three side lengths are fixed and satisfy the triangle inequality, exactly one triangle (up to congruence) is determined.
condition: Given three side lengths that satisfy the triangle inequality.
response: Conclude that exactly one unique triangle can be formed.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given side lengths 3 in, 4 in, and 6 in, these determine a single unique triangle.

title: Supplementary Angles Sum to 180°
description: Recognize that two angles forming a straight line sum to 180 degrees.
condition: Two adjacent angles form a straight line.
response: Write an equation adding the two angle measures and set equal to 180°.
label: Principle / Model
example:
If angles are $b$ and $c$ on a straight line, then
$$b + c = 180.$$

title: Surface Area of a Prism
description: Find total surface area by summing the areas of all faces (two bases and lateral faces).
condition: Given a prism with known base area(s) and lateral face dimensions.
response: Add areas of two bases and all lateral faces to get total surface area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If base area = 1,204.5 and prism height = 35, and perimeter of base = 157, then lateral area = 157 \times 35 and total surface area = 2(1,204.5) + 157 \times 35.
notes: Lateral area may be computed as perimeter × prism height.

title: Triangle Inequality Theorem
description: The sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the third side.
condition: Given three side lengths to determine if a triangle can be formed.
response: Check whether each pair sums to more than the third side.
label: Principle
example: Since $1 + 3 = 4 < 6$, a triangle with sides 1, 3, and 6 cannot exist.

title: Vertical Angles are Equal
description: Opposite angles formed by intersecting lines are congruent.
condition: Two angles are vertical (formed by intersecting straight lines and opposite each other).
label: Principle
example: If one vertical angle is $30^\circ$, the opposite angle $x = 30^\circ$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Vertical Cross-Section of Rectangular Pyramid
description: A slice through the vertex perpendicular to the base of a rectangular pyramid forms a triangular cross-section.
condition: A plane passes through the vertex of a rectangular pyramid and is perpendicular to the base.
response: Identify the cross-section as a triangle whose height equals the pyramid’s height.
label: Concept
example: A perpendicular slice through the vertex produces a triangular cross-section.
